title: Acute Ischemic Stroke with M1 Occlusion

learning_objectives.description: Perform systematic initial assessment of acute stroke patient including vital signs, glucose, and time of onset
Apply thrombolysis eligibility criteria including time window and contraindications
Interpret non-contrast CT to exclude hemorrhage before thrombolysis
Identify large vessel occlusion on CT angiography and determine thrombectomy eligibility
Manage blood pressure appropriately for thrombolysis candidacy

patient.presenting_complaint: Unable to speak and unable to move right arm

initial_presentation: A 70-year-old man presents in the emergency room after being found by his spouse unable to speak and unable to move his right arm. At admission he had right hemianopsia, his right arm was paretic, his leg has diminished strength against resistance, he was unable to speak or follow commands.
interaction_prompt: You are the emergency physician receiving this patient. You can ask for clinical information, order investigations, or initiate treatments. For example: 'What are the patient's vital signs?'

clinical_time_window.time_pressure_rationale: Patient last seen well 3 hours ago. Thrombolysis window is 4.5 hours from symptom onset, leaving approximately 90 minutes to complete assessment and initiate treatment.

stages.name: Emergency Room Assessment and Acute Management
stages.initial_state: Patient has just arrived by private vehicle with spouse. He appears in distress, unable to communicate effectively.

decision_points.context: The patient has just arrived in the emergency department with acute onset right-sided weakness and speech difficulty, accompanied by his partner.

available_actions.finding: Blood pressure is 210/105 mmHg
available_actions.rationale: Blood pressure must be below 185/110 mmHg for thrombolysis eligibility

available_actions.finding: Heart rate is 87 bpm
Respiratory rate is 16 breaths/min
Temperature is 36.6°C
available_actions.rationale: Fever may indicate infection or affect prognosis

available_actions.finding: Oxygen saturation is 99% on room air
The patient has atrial fibrillation rhythm with 85 bpm
available_actions.rationale: Identifies rhythm abnormalities and potential cardioembolic source

available_actions.finding: Blood glucose at presentation is 105 mg/dL
available_actions.rationale: Hypoglycemia can mimic stroke and must be excluded

available_actions.finding: ECG shows atrial fibrillation
available_actions.rationale: Confirms rhythm but should not delay acute treatment

available_actions.finding: Blood samples collected and sent for analysis
available_actions.rationale: Results needed but should not delay imaging or treatment in most cases

available_actions.finding: The patient is not taking any medication, including antiplatelets or anticoagulants

available_actions.description: Determine when patient was last seen well
available_actions.finding: The patient was last seen well 3 hours before admission
available_actions.rationale: Critical for determining thrombolysis eligibility window

available_actions.finding: The patient has no known cardiovascular risk factors including hypertension, diabetes, dyslipidemia, cigarette smoking or alcohol consumption
available_actions.rationale: Identifies contraindications and informs secondary prevention

available_actions.finding: Weight is 80 kg
Right hemianopsia, right arm paresis, diminished leg strength against resistance, unable to speak or follow commands. NIHSS score is 7.
NIHSS score is 7
available_actions.rationale: Quantifies stroke severity for treatment decisions

available_actions.finding: Pre-morbid modified Rankin score is 0 (no symptoms)
The patient has no known allergies

available_actions.description: Ask about previous surgeries or trauma
available_actions.finding: No recent major trauma, traumatic brain injury, major surgery, major bleeding, or gastrointestinal bleeding

available_actions.description: Ask about previous stroke or MI
available_actions.finding: No recent stroke or myocardial infarction
available_actions.rationale: Recent stroke is a contraindication to thrombolysis

available_actions.finding: Arterial blood gas was unremarkable
Troponin is negative

decision_points.feedback_on_completion: Initial vital signs assessment complete. Blood pressure is elevated at 210/105 mmHg which exceeds the 185/110 mmHg threshold for thrombolysis. Atrial fibrillation is noted. Blood glucose is normal. Imaging is now indicated.

decision_points.context: Initial clinical assessment is complete. The patient has a significant neurological deficit with stable vital signs.

available_actions.finding: Non-contrast CT scan showed no signs of acute ischemic stroke or hemorrhage
ECG shows atrial fibrillation
Blood samples collected and sent for analysis
The patient is not taking any medication, including antiplatelets or anticoagulants

available_actions.description: Determine when patient was last seen well
available_actions.finding: The patient was last seen well 3 hours before admission
The patient has no known cardiovascular risk factors including hypertension, diabetes, dyslipidemia, cigarette smoking or alcohol consumption
Weight is 80 kg

decision_points.feedback_on_completion: CT head excludes hemorrhage and shows no early ischemic changes. The patient remains a potential thrombolysis candidate. Blood pressure management and vascular imaging are indicated.

decision_points.context: CT confirms no haemorrhage. The patient's blood pressure is 192/108 mmHg and neurological deficit persists.

available_actions.finding: Labetalol/nitrates/urapidil administered. Blood pressure reduced to 150/75 mmHg
available_actions.rationale: Blood pressure must be below 185/110 mmHg for thrombolysis eligibility

available_actions.finding: The patient was last seen well 3 hours before admission
Blood samples collected and sent for analysis
The patient has no known cardiovascular risk factors including hypertension, diabetes, dyslipidemia, cigarette smoking or alcohol consumption
The patient is not taking any medication, including antiplatelets or anticoagulants
CT angiography showed a proximal occlusion of the M1 segment of left middle cerebral artery
available_actions.rationale: Identifies large vessel occlusion for thrombectomy consideration

available_actions.finding: ECG shows atrial fibrillation
Weight is 80 kg

decision_points.feedback_on_completion: Blood pressure is now within target range. Patient is within the 4.5 hour window, has no contraindications to thrombolysis. CT angiography shows M1 occlusion, making the patient a candidate for both thrombolysis and thrombectomy.

decision_points.context: Blood pressure is now controlled at 178/96 mmHg. The patient remains within the treatment time window with no contraindications identified so far.

available_actions.finding: Thrombolysis initiated. Alteplase 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg): 10% bolus over 1 minute, remaining 90% infused over 60 minutes. Alternative: Tenecteplase 0.25 mg/kg single bolus (max 25 mg).
available_actions.rationale: Standard of care for eligible patients within 4.5 hours of symptom onset

available_actions.finding: ECG shows atrial fibrillation
CT angiography shows proximal occlusion of M1 segment of left middle cerebral artery

decision_points.feedback_on_completion: Thrombolysis has been initiated. Given the M1 occlusion, the patient should also be evaluated for mechanical thrombectomy.

decision_points.context: Thrombolysis is running. Blood analysis results are now available for review.

available_actions.finding: Blood analysis available 1 hour after admission: unremarkable, coagulation normal, platelets 250,000
ECG shows atrial fibrillation
CT angiography shows proximal occlusion of M1 segment of left middle cerebral artery
available_actions.rationale: Confirms large vessel occlusion requiring thrombectomy

decision_points.feedback_on_completion: Laboratory results confirm no contraindication. M1 occlusion confirmed on CTA. Patient is a candidate for mechanical thrombectomy.

decision_points.context: CT angiography confirms occlusion of the left M1 segment. The patient has received IV thrombolysis and remains clinically stable.

available_actions.finding: Neuroradiology contacted to perform mechanical thrombectomy
available_actions.rationale: Patients with anterior circulation LVO may benefit from both thrombolysis and thrombectomy

decision_points.feedback_on_completion: Thrombectomy arranged. The patient will undergo mechanical thrombectomy for the M1 occlusion. This dual approach (thrombolysis plus thrombectomy) is recommended for eligible patients with large vessel occlusion.

stages.progression_criteria: Thrombolysis initiated and thrombectomy arranged for M1 occlusion

all_optional.finding: In terms of autonomy, the patient has a modified Rankin score of 0.
Right hemianopsia, his right arm was paretic, his leg has diminished strength against resistance, he was unable to speak or follow commands. NIHSS score is 7.
Gasometry / arterial blood gas (ABG) was unremarkable.
The patient has no known allergies.

No recent major trauma, no recent traumatic brain injury, no recent major surgery, no recent major bleeding, no recent gastrointestinal bleeding. The patient has no other medical conditions or relevant antecedents.
No recent stroke, no recent myocardial infarction.

debriefing.clinical_reasoning: Thrombolysis is recommended for patients with acute ischemic stroke within 4.5 hours after symptom onset and no contraindication. The benefit of thrombolysis is time-dependent, with earlier treatment associated with better outcomes. Rapid assessment of eligibility criteria is essential.

common_errors.description: Delaying CT imaging for non-urgent investigations
common_errors.consequence: Delayed identification of hemorrhage or ischemia, narrowing the treatment window for thrombolysis

common_errors.description: Waiting for blood results before thrombolysis in patients without history of coagulopathy
common_errors.consequence: Unnecessary delay of thrombolysis reduces treatment benefit, as efficacy is time-dependent

common_errors.description: Failing to obtain time last seen well early in assessment
common_errors.consequence: Inability to determine thrombolysis eligibility, potentially excluding eligible patients or treating ineligible ones

debriefing.key_learning: Large vessel occlusion requires dual intervention consideration
debriefing.clinical_reasoning: Patients with anterior circulation large vessel occlusion may be eligible for both thrombolysis and thrombectomy. These treatments are complementary, not mutually exclusive. Thrombolysis should not be delayed for thrombectomy, and thrombectomy should not be delayed waiting for thrombolysis response.

common_errors.description: Assuming thrombolysis alone is sufficient for large vessel occlusion
common_errors.consequence: Persistent large vessel occlusion despite thrombolysis, leading to larger infarct volume and worse outcomes

common_errors.description: Delaying thrombectomy referral while waiting for thrombolysis effect
common_errors.consequence: Increased infarct size as collateral circulation fails over time, reducing benefit of thrombectomy

common_errors.description: Not performing CT angiography in suspected LVO
common_errors.consequence: Missed large vessel occlusion, preventing timely thrombectomy referral

debriefing.key_learning: Blood pressure management is essential for thrombolysis eligibility
debriefing.clinical_reasoning: Blood pressure must be below 185/110 mmHg for thrombolysis. Aggressive but careful blood pressure reduction with IV agents (labetalol, nitrates, urapidil) should be initiated promptly in hypertensive patients who are otherwise thrombolysis candidates.

common_errors.description: Failing to lower blood pressure before thrombolysis
common_errors.consequence: Patient remains ineligible for thrombolysis due to elevated blood pressure, missing the treatment window

common_errors.description: Excessive blood pressure reduction causing hypotension
common_errors.consequence: Hypotension may worsen cerebral ischemia in the penumbral zone

common_errors.description: Using oral antihypertensives instead of IV agents in acute setting
common_errors.consequence: Oral agents have unpredictable onset and cannot be titrated rapidly, risking inadequate or delayed blood pressure control

debriefing.clinical_reasoning: The presence of atrial fibrillation indicates a likely cardioembolic source and will influence secondary prevention strategy. While not affecting acute treatment decisions, this finding should be documented for subsequent anticoagulation planning.

common_errors.description: Initiating anticoagulation in the acute phase before repeat imaging
common_errors.consequence: Risk of hemorrhagic transformation of the ischemic infarct

common_errors.description: Failing to document rhythm for secondary prevention planning
common_errors.consequence: Missed opportunity to plan appropriate secondary prevention with anticoagulation

key_takeaways: Thrombolysis is recommended within 4.5 hours of symptom onset without contraindications
Blood pressure must be ≤185/110 mmHg for thrombolysis eligibility
Large vessel occlusion patients may benefit from both thrombolysis and thrombectomy
Blood analysis should be checked before thrombolysis only if there is history of coagulopathy
ECG should be performed but should not delay acute treatment
